coredata.dc:title: Eu-doped chitosan carbon dot/dendritic silver nanostructure-based biosensor for BRAF detection in thyroid cancer exosome
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shijie
Li, Huixin
Wang, Peilin
Ma, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor has been constructed with Eu-doped chitosan carbon dot (Eu: Cs dot) and dendritic silver nanostructure. Firstly, Eu: Cs dots were synthesized as ECL tags, which not only possessed exceptional electrochemical characters and intense luminescence, but also can reacted with H2O2 as co-reactant to generate ECL signal. Furthermore, dendritic silver nanostructure with conductive gel was successfully prepared as sensing interface. The dendritic silver nanostructure with conductive gel displayed the ideal conductivity, good electrochemical stability, which can enhance the ECL intensity of Eu: CS dots. Finally, BRAF V600E from thyroid cancer exosome in the clinic eluate can be captured and detected. The results indicated the relative association between BRAF V600E mutation and lymphatic metastasis, which can be used in assessing thyroid cancer progression for advancing clinical diagnosis applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Eu-doped chitosan carbon dot
ECL biosensor
Dendritic silver nanostructure
Conductive gel
Exosome detection
Thyroid cancer diagnosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical deposition of the dendritic silver nanostructure
ECL performance of Eu: CS dots
Characterization of dendritic silver nanostructure/conductive gel-based interface

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (I) The synthesis method of Eu: CS dots. (II) The preparation process of the sensing interface based on the dendritic silver nanostructure and the conductive gel. (III) the constructed ECL biosensor for detecting BRAF V600E in the exosome.

(A) TEM image, (B) FT-IR spectrum and (C) the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of Eu: CS dots. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) CS dots and (b) Eu: CS dots. The effect of (E) the Eu ion concentration and (F) synthesis temperature on the fluorescence performance of Eu: CS dots.

(A) The effect of Eu ion concentration on the ECL intensity of Eu: CS dots. (B) The ECL response of Eu: CS dots with different concentration of H2O2 as co-reactant. (C) ECL-potential curves and (D) ECL spectra of (a) CS dots and (b) Eu: CS dots.

(A) The SEM image of dendritic silver nanostructure. (B) EIS of (a) GCE, (b) GCE/dendritic silver nanostructure and (c) GCE/dendritic silver nanostructure/conductive gel. (C) The cyclic voltammogram of the dendritic silver nanostructure (a) without and (b) with conductive gel in the H2O2 solution. The insert was the cyclic voltammogram of GCE. (D) The ECL response of (a) Eu: CS dots, (b) Eu: CS dots/the dendritic silver nanostructure, (c) Eu: CS dots on the dendritic silver nanostructure/conductive gel. (E) UV–vis spectrum of the dendritic silver nanostructure and ECL spectrum of Eu: CS dots.

(A) EIS of (a) GCE, (b) GCE/dendritic silver nanostructure/conductive gel, (c) (b)/capture DNA, and (d) GCE/dendritic silver nanostructure/conductive gel/DNA duplex structure/Eu: CS dots. (B) The relationship between the ECL intensity and different concentration of BRAF V600E. (C) The corresponding linear relationship for target DNA detection. (D) ECL intensities of (a) BRAF V600E, (b) miRNA-221, (c) miRNA-135b, (d) miRNA-585-3p (10 nM). Error bars represent the standard deviations of three independent measurements. (E) Stability of the ECL sensing system. (F) Size distribution of exosomes detected by NTA. The insert was SEM image of the exosomes extracted from the puncture eluates of thyroid tumor microenvironment.
The nucleotide sequences of the oligonucleotides in the work.
The nucleotide sequences of the oligonucleotides in the work.

head.ce:title: Eu-doped chitosan carbon dot/dendritic silver nanostructure-based biosensor for BRAF detection in thyroid cancer exosome

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Thyroid Surgery, China−Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China−Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Thyroid Surgery, China−Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor has been constructed with Eu-doped chitosan carbon dot (Eu: Cs dot) and dendritic silver nanostructure. Firstly, Eu: Cs dots were synthesized as ECL tags, which not only possessed exceptional electrochemical characters and intense luminescence, but also can reacted with H2O2 as co-reactant to generate ECL signal. Furthermore, dendritic silver nanostructure with conductive gel was successfully prepared as sensing interface. The dendritic silver nanostructure with conductive gel displayed the ideal conductivity, good electrochemical stability, which can enhance the ECL intensity of Eu: CS dots. Finally, BRAF V600E from thyroid cancer exosome in the clinic eluate can be captured and detected. The results indicated the relative association between BRAF V600E mutation and lymphatic metastasis, which can be used in assessing thyroid cancer progression for advancing clinical diagnosis applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Thyroid carcinoma is the most common endocrine malignancy with the rinsing incidence rates. The papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC) is the most common histological subtype of thyroid cancer. Although most PTC patients have a good prognosis, it is noteworthy that there is lymph node metastasis in 20–90 % of PTC patients. The lymphatic metastasis significantly increases the probability of postoperative parathyroid injury and laryngeal nerve injury in patients [1,2]. The kinase-activating mutations in the V-Raf murine sarcoma viral oncogene homolog B1(BRAF) mutations are found in almost 80 % of patients with recurrent metastatic PTC [3]. Especially, the single amino acid substitution from valine to glutamic acid at codon 600 (V600E), accounts for almost 90 % of all BRAF mutations. In PTC, BRAF V600E mutation has been associated with lymph node metastasis, extrathyroidal extension, distant metastasis, recurrent/persistent disease and worst prognosis. So, the molecular testing of BRAF mutation is important to both thyroid cancer diagnosis and to guide effective treatment strategy. The developments in the assay techniques for various molecular biomarkers (e.g., DNA methylation, various microRNAs and long non-coding RNAs) are becoming the diagnostic, prognostic, and predictive tools in thyroid cancer [4,5]. However, the analysis capability of these mutations remains poor due to the complex biomatrix, dynamic changes and the low abundance content. Most genetic alterations occur in a limited number of genes and led to a low mutation burden. Recent researches have shown that plasma exosomes containing DNA and microRNAs derived from cancer cells have potential to act as non-invasive biomarkers [6–8]. Therefore, the non-invasive liquid biopsy based on tumor microenvironment and plasma-derived exosomes is expected to improve the accuracy of mutation diagnosis.

As a powerful liquid biopsy technique, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is based on a unique emission process without external excitation light, which is triggered by an electrochemical reaction at the electrode surface [9,10]. ECL with advantages of superb sensitivity, wide dynamic ranges, near-zero background noise and fast response has been widely applied in clinical assay, environmental analysis, medical diagnostics, and food security [11–13]. Especially, many ECL biosensors have been developed for nucleic acid analysis with high sensitivity and specificity. These proposed ECL sensing systems have showed great potential in fundamental research and clinical diagnosis [14,15]. Because the emitters are the main components in the ECL sensing system, substantial efforts have been devoted to exploring highly efficient nano-ECL emitters (e.g. quantum dots, metal nanoclusters, and metal–organic frameworks) over the past decades [16–18]. Among various nanomaterials, carbon dots have recently received widespread attention as luminescence probe due to the attractive photophysical and electrochemical properties [19,20]. Carbon dots are a kind of nonmetallic quantum dots with small particle size distribution, wonderful stability, excellent biocompatibility, rich sources and easy functionalization [21]. Because the organic precursors of carbon dots are mainly limited to small toxic aromatic compounds, the development of natural and economical precursors for the large-scale production of carbon dots possesses great potential in the actual applications. Many raw materials have been used to synthesize carbon dots, such as eggshell membranes, citric acid, and glucose. Chitosan is a natural non-synthetic amino polysaccharide comprising β-(1,4)- linked 2-amino-2deoxy-D-glucose residues, which is derived by the deacetylation of chitin as a derivative of marine wastes [22,23]. Chitosan possesses multiple functional groups, including acetamido, amino, and hydroxyl groups. On the one hand, because of abundant hydroxyl (–OH) and amino (−NH2) functional groups, chitosan can be used as a precursor for the preparation of carbon dots [24,25]. On the other hand, the abundant nitrogen content (∼7 wt%) can be used as the self-doped N elements to increase the surface-state defects of carbon dots to improve the luminescence property [26]. However, the luminescence signal of the chitosan carbon dots is still restricted by inferior controllability and poor reproducibility. Importantly, unlike the photoluminescence emission, ECL originates from the electron transfer on the surface of carbon dots. The ECL performance of carbon dots is dependent on the modulating the surface defects. Until now, the poor ECL yield of chitosan carbon dots was largely limited by the analysis application. It should be noticed that the synthesis of carbon dots as ECL emitters by the cost-effective and eco-friendly pathway is still a challenge.

In this work, we synthesized a novel Eu-doped chitosan-based carbon dots (Eu: CS dots) as ECL emitters and developed an ECL biosensor for BRAF detection as shown in Fig. 1 . Microwave pyrolysis approach was used as the well-established method for chitosan carbon dots synthesis. The uniform heating of microwaves can form particles with a uniform size distribution. As a result, the polymer chains of chitosan are conducive to the construction of continuous graphite structure in carbon dots. Eu ion was used as the doping heteroatom to improve the ECL performance of carbon dots through the surface defect modulation. The as-prepared Eu: CS dots possessed high ECL yield and good electrochemical activity. Furthermore, a new sensing interface for detecting BRAF V600E was constructed by coating the dendritic silver nanostructure with the conductive gel. The dendritic silver nanostructure was electrodeposited on the electrode to enhance the electron transfer. And the conductive gel was prepared with PEDOT: PSS, SBMA, and PEGDA, which not only remained the good conductivity of the sensing interface, but also reduced the oxidation of the dendritic silver nanostructure [27]. Due to the large active surface area for the analyte immobilization and the high conductivity, the dendritic silver nanostructure with the conductive gel enhanced the ECL intensity of Eu: CS dots. In the sensing system, the BRAF V600E released from exosome can be can be captured and identified. The detection results demonstrated that the sensing strategy achieved the ultrasensitive and specific detection of exosome, providing a potential clinical diagnosis method for PTC lymphatic metastasis.

Chitosan, N-ethyl-N'-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Silver nitrate (AgNO3), Eu(NO)3·6H2O, KNO3, sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), hydrogen diphosphate sodium (Na2HPO4), sodium phosphate (Na3PO4), poly(3,4ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS), [2-(methacryloyloxy)ethyl]dimethyl-(3-sulfopropyl) (SBMA), and poly(ethylene glycol) diacrylate (PEGDA) were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used without further purification. The oligonucleotides were synthesized and purified by Qingke Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China), as shown in Table 1 . The TransExo cell media exosome Kit and EasyPure miRNA Kit were purchased from TransGen Biotech Inc. (Beijing, China).

TEM images were obtained with JEM-2100F field emission electron microscope operating at 200 kV acceleration voltages. The SEM images were obtained on Hitachi SU8020 field emission scanning electron microscope. Fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption were respectively acquired by F-2700 Fluorescence spectrophotometer and Hitachi UH5300 UV–visible spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was measured on a Thermo Nicolet 360 FTIR spectrometer using KBr pellets. The cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and electrochemical deposition process were performed using CHI 660B electrochemical workstation with conventional three-electrode system. The ECL measurements were carried out with CHI 660B electrochemical work station, H10682-210 photomultiplier tubes and the c8855-01 signal recording unit from Hamamatsu Photonics Co., Ltd.

0.3 g chitosan was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water. The solution was stirred continuously until became clear. Then, Eu(NO)3·6H2O was added into the above solution. The mixed solution was heated under 150 °C for 20 min in the microwave reactor (1000 W). After centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min, the yellow product was obtained as Eu: CS dots. After activated with 30 μL10 mM EDC and 10 μL 10 mM NHS, the probe DNA was incubated with Eu: CS dots for two hours to form Eu: CS dots/probe DNA. The solution was dialyzed for 4 h to remove the unreacted probe DNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical deposition of the dendritic silver nanostructure
ce:section.ce:para: The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with aluminum oxide powder and cleaned by ultrasonication in ultrapure water and ethanol respectively. Then the cleaned GCE was immersed in the mixed solution containing 5 mM AgNO3 and 0.1 M KNO3. The electrochemical deposition process was performed in three-electrode system via the constant voltage at −0.5 V and the deposition time was 400 s. After the dendritic Ag nanostructure was obtained, the electrode surface was rinsed with water and dried.

0.3 g SBMA, 5 µL PEGDA, and 20 µL PEDOT: PSS were mixed and dissolved in 1 mL water to form the conductive gel. The obtained conductive gel was dropped on the electrode to cover the dendritic Ag nanostructure. 6 μL of 10 µM capture DNA was dropped on the modified electrode surface and incubated for 12 h. After washed with PBS, the unbound sites were blocked by MCH (5 μL, 1 mM) for 1 h. The electrode surface was incubated with different concentrations of target DNA at 37 °C for 2 h. After washing with buffer, the electrode was incubated with 50 μL of Eu: CS dots/probe DNA at 37 °C for another 2 h. The ECL detection was performed using a 60 mM H2O2 solution as coreactant with a scanning voltage of −2.5 V ∼ 0 V.

The pathological samples of thyroid cancer were obtained from the China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University. The pressure needle was used to puncture the thyroid tissue under direct vision and rinse with 1 mL of normal saline to obtain the tumor microenvironment eluates. The exosomes in the eluate were extracted by the precipitation solution from the Exosome Kit. Then, DNA was extracted from exosome. 500 μL DNA lysate was added to the exosome solution (containing 0.5 mg/ml protease K). Then, the sample solution was heated in water bath for 4 h. After the extraction, anhydrous ethanol was added to the solution to precipitate DNA. After the centrifugation at 2–8 °C for 15 min., the obtained DNA was dissolved and detected. Finally, the ECL signal was detected with a three-electrode system in the sensing system, including GCE as the working electrode, platinum wire and Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) electrodes are used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, the Eu: CS dots were successfully prepared. The dispersed Eu: CS dots exhibited uniform particle size with ca.10 nm. In the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum (Fig. 2B) of Eu: CS dots, the O–H deformation vibration peak was observed at 3400 cm−1. The peak at 2900 cm−1 represented C-H. And there were symmetric vibration peaks of C = O at 1650 cm−1. The peak at 1400 cm−1 was corresponding to N-H. And C-N vibrations at 1300 cm−1 can be also observed. The above results also indicated that Eu: CS dots were successfully synthesized. As shown in Fig. 2C, when the maximum excitation wavelength was at 360 nm, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of Eu: CS dots was at 450 nm. The insert image displayed the bright blue fluorescence of Eu: CS dots under UV light. In Fig. 2D, the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy of dots without Eu-doping had a peak at 270 nm. The distinct absorption peak at 424 nm was attribute to the n-electron or π-electron leaping into π*.[28] While the doping Eu3+ caused a ca. 30 nm redshift in the UV–vis absorption spectrum from 270 nm to 300 nm. It was due to the increased quantum dot radius after doping and the decreased energy gap.[29] The quality of Eu: CS dots was directly dependent on the concentration of the doping ions. Fig. 2E showed the change of fluorescence intensity of Eu: CS dots with different Eu ions concentration. During the doping process, the introduction of the large radius of Eu ions provided new defects due to lattice distortion. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of Eu: CS dots enhanced gradually with the increased Eu ions amounts from 0 to 5 mg/mL. But excessive Eu ions (much than 10 mg/mL) led to the quenching of fluorescence intensity. It was caused by the increased nonradiative transitions of high concentration of Eu ions. Furthermore, different synthesis temperature also had important effects on the fluorescence performance of Eu: CS dots (Fig. 2F). The fluorescence intensity of Eu: CS dots increased with the increased temperature from 120 °C to 150 °C. Then, the higher reaction temperature over 150 °C destabilized the material structure. The Eu: CS dots showed low fluorescence intensity when the synthesized temperature was 180 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL performance of Eu: CS dots
ce:section.ce:para:
In Fig. 3 A, the concentration of doping Eu ion also had a great effect on the electrochemical and ECL properties of Eu: CS dots. With the Eu ion concentration increasing, the intensity of ECL enhanced gradually from ca. 1100 to ca. 6600 (a.u.). Similar with the fluorescence performance, the maximum ECL was recorded when the doping Eu ion concentration reached 5 mg/mL. The results indicated there was the same luminescence radiation center in Eu: CS dots for the fluorescence and ECL. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, H2O2 can be used as co-reactant to generate reductive-oxidation ECL of Eu: CS dots. The low concentration of H2O2 was insufficient in the ECL reaction. Only a few free radicals of H2O2 generated the low ECL intensity with Eu: CS dots. When the concentration of H2O2 was increased to 60 mM, the strongest ECL signal was observed. A relatively high concentration of H2O2 (>60 mM) can inhibit the excited state of Eu: CS dots and quench ECL signal significantly. Fig. 3C showed that the ECL excitation potential of Eu: CS dots and CS dots were both − 2.2 V. Compared with CS dots, Eu: CS dots had a 4-fold enhancement of ECL intensity. Therefore, the above results clearly displayed the excellent ECL performance of Eu: CS dots. Fig. 3D showed that the ECL peak of CS dots and Eu: CS dots were located at 585 nm and 620 nm, respectively. The redshift of ca. 35 nm in the ECL peak of Eu: CS dots was caused by the introduction of new defects and the reduction of the band gap width with the doping Eu ion. The ECL mechanism of the Eu: CS dots with H2O2 can be described as follows:
(1)
H 2
O 2
+
e -
→ H
O ∙
+ O
H -
(2)
HO · + Eu :
S dot
+
e -
→ E u :
C S
d o t ∗ + O
H -
(3)
Eu : C S d o t
∗
→ E u : C S d o t + h ν
The above results have shown that the Eu: CS dots was ideal ECL nano-emitter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of dendritic silver nanostructure/conductive gel-based interface
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical deposition was a reliable and highly efficient pathway to construct nanostructure, which controlled the nucleation and growth process via the electrochemical deposition conditions. In the preparation of Ag nanostructure with the applied voltage, the intensity of the electric field affected the diffusion of silver ions and induced the growth of nanostructures along the direction of the electric field. Meanwhile, the high concentration of silver ions led to the vertical growth of the dendritic-like layer. Therefore, the Ag structure grew from small nanoparticles to layered dendritic-like structures with branches. The SEM image in Fig. 4 A showed dendritic silver nanostructure was formed successfully on the electrode surface. Then, the transfer resistance (Rct) recorded in Fig. 4B was 355 Ω due to the good conductivity of silver nanostructure. In order to improve the stability of the dendritic silver nanostructure, the conductive gel was prepared with PEDOT: PSS, SBMA, and PEGDA and used to modify the electrode surface. The gel not only kept the good conductivity of the sensing interface, but also reduced the oxidation of the dendritic silver nanostructure. When the conductive gel was coated on the dendritic silver nanostructure, the value of Rct slight decreased to 286 Ω. As shown in Fig. 4C, in comparison with GCE, there was obvious redox peaks in the cyclic voltammetry curves of the dendritic silver nanostructure in the H2O2 solution. The redox reaction generated between the dendritic silver nanostructure and H2O2 during the cyclic voltammogram process due to the high electrochemical activity of silver nanostructure. When the conductive gel was modified on the electrode, the redox current decreased. It indicated the gel protected silver nanostructure and decreased the reaction degree with H2O2. The stability of the dendritic silver nanostructure was improved. ECL tests were performed to investigate the work of dendritic silver nanostructure/conductive gel-based interface. In Fig. 4D, when Eu: CS dots attached to the dendritic silver nanostructure, the ECL signals decreased from ca. 3920 to ca. 3372 (a.u.). On the one hand, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the dendritic silver nanostructure and the ECL emission spectrum of Eu: CS dots exhibited the spectral overlap in Fig. 4E. It indicated the dendritic silver nanostructure can work as a strong energy receptor. When donor Eu: CS dots closed to the dendritic silver nanostructure, the resonance energy transfer occurred ultimately. On the other hand, the dendritic silver nanostructure with high electrochemical activity further consumed large amounts of H2O2, which was used as co-reactant in the ECL reaction. As a result, the ECL signal of Eu: CS dots was quenched. The modified conductive gel effectively enlarged the distance between Eu: CS dots and the dendritic silver nanostructure, and improved the stability of the dendritic silver nanostructure. So, the ECL intensity of Eu: CS dots was amplified by more than 2.3 times. The resulting dendritic silver nanostructure/conductive gel directly acted as the sensing interface for subsequent detection.

The constructed sensing system was used to detect the expression level of BRAF V600E. The EIS results obtained from the construction process of the ECL sensing system were displayed in Fig. 5 A. When dendritic silver nanostructure/conductive gel was modified on the electrode surface, it provided high conductivity with low Rct. Due to the dielectric property of nucleic acid, the Rct value increased to 407 Ω when the capture was connected on the sensing interface. Next, as BRAF V600E and Eu: CS dots-probe DNA hybridized sequentially in the sensing process, the Rct value gradually increased from 530 Ω to 630 Ω. The above results demonstrated that the sensing system has been successfully constructed. In the sensing process, the ECL responses were recorded for the quantitative detection of BRAF V600E in Fig. 5B. And the correlation between ECL signals and the logarithm of BRAF V600E concentration was presented in Fig. 5C. It displayed a linear range from 1 fM to 100 nM. The regression equation was I = 495Log c + 7596 with the correlation coefficient R2 of 0.96. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.5 fM. To evaluate the specificity of the proposed biosensor, different nucleic acids were added as interference. The results showed that there were low ECL signals of these interference in Fig. 5D. The intra- and inter-assays results in Fig. 5E indicated the ECL sensing system had good stability. The puncture eluates from thyroid patients were collected and further detected. The exosomes in puncture eluates were characterized by SEM in the insert of Fig. 5F. The exosomes presented a typical cup-like morphology. And the size and concentration of exosomes were measured by nanoparticle tracking analysis (NTA). The results in Fig. 5F showed the size of exosomes was ca. 150 nm, which was consistent with the SEM characterization. The results indicated that exosomes had been obtained from thyroid tumor microenvironment successfully. After extraction from exosome, the concentration of BRAF V600E was detected and summarized in Table 2 . The detection results showed the expression of BRAF V600E in the sample which have already undergone lymphatic metastasis. It indicated that the proposed ECL sensing system had the potential to predict thyroid metastasis by analyzing the expression level of exosomal BRAF V600E.

In summary, a novel biosensor composed of the luminescence Eu: CS dots and dendritic silver nanostructure/conductive gel has been designed for detection of BRAF V600E in the exosome. The luminescence Eu: CS dots were used as the ECL emitters. In the sensing system, dendritic silver nanostructure/conductive gel-based sensing interface provided a unique substrate for the sensitive detection of target DNA. The sensing interface had excellent conductivity and stability to achieve the sensitive determination of target DNA ranging from 1 fM to 100 pM, with detection limit of 5 fM. Finally, the expression level of exosomal BRAF V600E from thyroid cancer has been successfully detected. The dendritic silver nanostructure/conductive gel-based sensing platform had great potential in the detection of exosomes for cancer diagnosis.
The use of human tissue samples has been approved by the ethics committee of the China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University (No. 2019-NSFC-051).

Shijie Li: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Huixin Li: Investigation. Peilin Wang: Validation, Methodology. Qiang Ma: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Jilin Province health research talents special project（NO:2021SCZ15）.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of a nomogram for central lymph node metastasis in papillary thyroid cancer based on gender grouping
Eur. Arch. Otorhinolaryngol.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Growth of robust carbon nitride films by double crystallization with exceptionally boosted electrochemiluminescence for visual DNA detection
